text: The lower esophageal sphincter or LES is a one-way valve that separates the esophagus from the stomach. When food or liquid is swallowed, the LES opens transiently to allow passage into the stomach. Otherwise, the sphincter remains closed to prevent stomach contents from flowing back up into the esophagus. However, for patients who suffer from reflux, the LES abnormally remains open allowing stomach secretions and contents to reflux up into the esophagus causing symptoms.

Should medical and lifestyle management fail to resolve reflux symptoms, such patients may be a candidate for surgical intervention which involves physically tightening the LES. The Nissen fundoplication or stomach wrap is the standard approach to surgically tighten the LES as shown here. The procedure essentially involves wrapping the top part of the stomach around the LES like a belt using tiny instruments inserted through small abdominal incisions. Here, actual surgical footage is shown depicting how a Nissen fundoplication is performed.

The surgeon manipulates the freed esophagus and stomach, looping a rubber band around the lower esophageal sphincter (LES). A white mesh is visible at the top, indicating a repaired hiatal hernia. The top part of the stomach is pushed behind and around the esophagus, moving it back and forth to ensure the wrap is loose enough to maintain adequate blood flow.

The surgeon sutures the stomach wrap, with the first suture connecting stomach tissue to stomach tissue. The second suture passes through the esophagus, and the final suture is placed through the lower esophageal sphincter (LES).
The surgeon completes the Nissen fundoplication, finalizing the stomach wrap around the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) to prevent reflux.
